coredata.dc:title: Achieving purple light excitable high-efficiency temperature-responsive dual- and single-mode afterglow in carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Jingran
Wu, Xiulan
Jiang, Xin
Li, Ming
He, Rongxing
Shen, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Afterglow materials including thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) have inspired extensive attention and research in anti-counterfeiting and sensing application prospects. However, a critical challenge currently remains within this afterglow materials only exhibit single-mode phosphorescence or delayed fluorescence with a persistent luminescence but low quantum yield. In this study, carbon dots composites with long-lived afterglow and ultra-high afterglow quantum yields of 193.5 ms and 35.07% were obtained by embedding the luminescent centers of ciprofloxacin into the formed rigid boric acid matrix by a one-step hydrothermal with purple light as the excitation wavelength. It is worth mentioning that the constructed composites exhibit dual-mode afterglow (DF and RTP) features at room temperature. Moreover, the carbon dots composites have distinctive temperature-responsive color-tunable properties and represent afterglow emission dominated by delayed fluorescence or phosphorescence at different temperatures. Based on the extraordinary temperature sensitivity, a single-mode ultra-long phosphorescence with a lifetime of up to 1.11 s is achieved at low temperatures. Additionally, as-prepared carbon dots composites exhibit the afterglow quantum yield of 40.97% in CB-VII, which is an ultra-high value for inorganic non-metallic materials. Furthermore, the resultant composites of carbon dots with new potential materials of encryption technology are designed for advanced information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Afterglow dual-mode carbon dots temperature-responsive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The morphological and photophysical characterization of controlled composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CB-I. (b) Size distribution of CB-I. (c) XRD patterns of CB-I powder and boric acid. (d) Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CB-I powder. (e) Full scan XPS spectrum of CB-I. XPS (f) C 1s, (g) O 1s, and (h) B 1s, (i) N 1s spectrum of CB-I.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum, fluorescence emission spectrum, and afterglow emission spectrum of CB-I. (b) Excitation-independent fluorescence emission spectra of CB-I. (c) Excitation-independent afterglow emission spectra of CB-I. (d) Temperature-dependent afterglow emission spectrum of CB-I under excitation at 392 nm. (e) Afterglow decay curves of CB-I were monitored at 438 nm and 490 nm with 392 nm excitation, respectively. (f) CIE coordinate diagram of CB-I, CB-II, CB-III, CB-IV, CB-V, CB-VI, and CB-VII. (g) Corresponding photographs of CB-I under conditions of sunlight, and 365 nm UV lamp on and off.

Temperature-dependent afterglow decay curves of CB-I at (a) 438 nm and (b) 490 nm. (c) Energy-level diagram of CB-I. (d) Afterglow decay curve of CB-I monitored at 490 nm under 80 K. (e) Corresponding photographs of CB-I powder under conditions of sunlight, and 365 nm UV lamp on and off under 80 K. (f) Luminescence images of CB-I at different temperatures.

(a) Corresponding photographs of CB-II, CB-III, CB-IV, CB-V, CB-VI, and CB-VII under conditions of sunlight, and 365 nm UV lamp on and off. (b) Fluorescence and (c) afterglow emission spectrum of CB-II, CB-III, CB-IV, CB-V, CB-VI, and CB-VII. Afterglow decay curves behaviors of the (d) DF and (e) RTP of CB-II, CB-III, CB-IV, CB-V, CB-VI, and CB-VII with 422 nm excitation, respectively.

(a) Corresponding photographs of CB-I, CB-II, CB-III, CB-IV, CB-V, CB-VI, and CB-VII on a piece of glass, respectively. (b) Digital photographs of information anticounterfeiting and encryption application of CB-I and CB-IV powder under 365 nm UV light. (c) Data encryption application of CB-I, CB-V, and CB-VII powder.
Illustration for the formation procedure of CB-I.
Photophysical data of CB-Ⅰ, CB-II, CB-III, CB-IV, CB-V, CB-VI, and CB-VII

head.ce:title: Achieving purple light excitable high-efficiency temperature-responsive dual- and single-mode afterglow in carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Afterglow materials including thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) have inspired extensive attention and research in anti-counterfeiting and sensing application prospects. However, a critical challenge currently remains within this afterglow materials only exhibit single-mode phosphorescence or delayed fluorescence with a persistent luminescence but low quantum yield. In this study, carbon dots composites with long-lived afterglow and ultra-high afterglow quantum yields of 193.5 ms and 35.07% were obtained by embedding the luminescent centers of ciprofloxacin into the formed rigid boric acid matrix by a one-step hydrothermal with purple light as the excitation wavelength. It is worth mentioning that the constructed composites exhibit dual-mode afterglow (DF and RTP) features at room temperature. Moreover, the carbon dots composites have distinctive temperature-responsive color-tunable properties and represent afterglow emission dominated by delayed fluorescence or phosphorescence at different temperatures. Based on the extraordinary temperature sensitivity, a single-mode ultra-long phosphorescence with a lifetime of up to 1.11 s is achieved at low temperatures. Additionally, as-prepared carbon dots composites exhibit the afterglow quantum yield of 40.97% in CB-VII, which is an ultra-high value for inorganic non-metallic materials. Furthermore, the resultant composites of carbon dots with new potential materials of encryption technology are designed for advanced information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications.

Carbon dots composites with long-lived afterglow and ultra-high afterglow quantum yields of 193.5 ms and 35.07% were obtained by embedding the luminescent centers of ciprofloxacin into the formed rigid boric acid matrix by a one-step hydrothermal with purple light as the excitation wavelength. The materials possesses distinctive afterglow emission dominated by delayed fluorescence or room temperature phosphorescence at different temperatures and enables color-tunable afterglow emission with temperature-sensitivity.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow luminescence material [1,2] is a kind of photoluminescence material whose luminescence is generated by the radiative decay of triplet excitons. Polymerization [3,4], host-guest doping [5], and crystallization [6,7] are several considerable strategies currently developed to limit molecular vibrational dissipations for reducing non-radiative transitions. Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) [8,9] materials with long-lived afterglow were developed and have continuously attracted the attention of researchers in programmable luminescent tags [10], fingerprinting [11], energy storage [12], and information encryption [5,7] due to its advantages of low toxicity [13], simple synthesis method [14], and excellent stability [15]. In addition, thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) emitter, which through reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) from the triplet state (T1) to the lowest singlet state (S1) and then return to the ground state (S0) are likewise potential candidates for achieving afterglow luminescence besides RTP. The unique thermal activation process of TADF can absorb heat and produce enhanced delayed fluorescence at higher temperatures. However, the efficiency and lifetime of the majority of afterglow materials in ambient conditions are inadequate, and is difficult for these materials to tune the color of the afterglow in response to environmental factors such as temperature. Therefore, it may be possible to modify the luminescence color by simple temperature adjustment if the materials feature simultaneously RTP and TADF dual-mode luminescence properties.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) [16–18] as novel afterglow nanomaterials have set off a research upsurge due to many advantages such as low toxicity, low cost, facile preparation, excellent biocompatibility [19,20], and have made great achievements in various fields, such as biological imaging [22], sensing [24,25], LED, etc. So far, superior RTP and TADF materials with long-lived afterglow have been developed by embedding CDs into a rigid matrix like PVA [27,28], PMMA, nano-silicon [29,30], and zeolite [31,32]. However, the majority of CDs afterglow materials obtained at present are single phosphorescence [33,34] or delayed fluorescence, and efficient dual-mode delayed fluorescence (DF) and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots composites are rare [35,36]. Obviously, afterglow materials based on modulating the relative intensity ratio of dual-mode afterglow emission have a greater level of security and higher resolution. Moreover, previous afterglow materials are mostly excited by ultraviolet light, which is complicated to excite by visible light [37]. Herein, a facile one-step hydrothermal is illustrated for achieving purple light excitable high-efficiency temperature-responsive color-tunable single-mode and dual-mode afterglow composites which are applied for the practice and application of long-lived afterglow materials in optical anti-counterfeiting and sensing fields.

In this study, bright afterglow emissions were detected from CB-I which was synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of ciprofloxacin and boric acid as shown in Scheme 1 . Surprisingly, the obtained composites possess stable dual-mode DF and RTP afterglow at ambient conditions, and the afterglow lifetime of CB-I under the excitation of visible purple light can reach 180.5 ms at 438 nm, and 193.5 ms at 490 nm. Meanwhile, CB-I composites exhibit significant blue fluorescence and cyan afterglow, with PLQY and afterglow quantum yield of up to 66.59% and 35.07%, which are expected figures for non-metallic and pure inorganic afterglow materials. Further, the ultra-long lifetime phosphorescence of 1.11 s can be achieved when the temperature drops to 80 K. Therefore, based on the temperature can be adjusted to fulfill single-mode and dual-mode conversion of the afterglow and represent temperature-responsive color-tunable afterglow performance. In addition, the controlled experiments of CB-I showed optical stability with an increase in ciprofloxacin feeding, and the afterglow quantum yield of CB-VII is as high as 40.97%, the ultra-high value superior to those involved in dual-mode afterglow carbon dot composites. At the same time, afterglow materials with high quantum efficiency are highly desirable and can be applied for information encryption, anti-counterfeiting, and other technologies.

Boric acid and ciprofloxacin were purchased from Macklin (China). Deionized (DI) water was used in the experiments. All reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial sources and used directly without further purification.

0.001 g ciprofloxacin and 3 g boric acid (BA) were placed in a beaker filled with 40 ml deionized water and dissolved by heating and ultrasonication. Then cover the beaker with a layer of foil and react in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h. After that, it was cooled down naturally and ground, and the CB-I powder was obtained.

CB-II, CB-III, CB-IV, CB-V, CB-VI, and CB-VII materials were prepared in the same way as described above. In order to make the difference between the samples clear and easy to distinguish, for the selection of the data it was decided to fix the mass of boric acid and to increase the mass of ciprofloxacin by different multiples of 5 based on the first set of data 0.001 g and the multiples were gradually increased to 2.5, 25, 75, 175, 375, 625 times of 0.001 g, which means that the mass of ciprofloxacin in the last six sets of data mass of 0.0025 g, 0.025 g, 0.075 g, 0.175 g, 0.375 g, and 0.725 g respectively. Further later ratios are not repeated due to solubility.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Rigaku Smart Lab. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were taken by BRUKER TENSOR 27010409 (Germany). UV–vis absorption spectrum was measured on a PERSEE TU-1901 UV–vis spectrometer. PL spectrum, afterglow spectrum, Fluorescence, and afterglow lifetime decay curves were recorded on a HORIBA Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence quantum yield and afterglow quantum yield were measured on the HORIBA Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a calibrated integrating sphere and a Quantaurus-QY measurement system. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a TA TGA Q50 instrument at a heating of rate 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere from 25 to 500 °C.

The morphological structure of CB-I was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images demonstrated the formation of the ciprofloxacin carbon dots dispersed uniformly in boric acid (Fig. 1 a) with an average diameter of 3.23 nm (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c illustrates the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CB-I, which identified an amorphous carbon phase [4,24,27] and distinct boric acid peaks at 14.6°, 28.0°, and 40.1°, respectively [38]. The chemical structure of CB-I composites was further carried out by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS). In the FTIR spectrum of the CB-I (Fig. 1d), the stretching vibration of O–H was ascribed to a noticeable absorption band at 3221 cm−1. Both 1466 cm−1 and 1192 cm−1 absorption bands can be explained by stretching vibrations of B–O and B–O–H, respectively. The band of B–C newly appeared in CB-I was at the position of 952 cm−1 [40]. The full-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) revealed the atomic percentages of C, O, N, and B are 24.93%, 62.25%, 2.55%, and 10.27%, respectively. C 1s XPS spectrum of CB-I (Fig. 1f) was deconvoluted into four distinct peaks that correspond to C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, C=O, and O–C=O bonds at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, 287.3 eV, and 288.6 eV, respectively. The XPS O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) exhibits three distinct peaks at 532.1 eV, 533.1 eV, and 534.2 eV, originating from C=O, C–O–C/C–O–B, and O–C=O/C–OH, respectively. Likewise, B 1s (Fig. 1h) also possesses the peaks of BCO2 (192.4 eV), B2O3 (193.3 eV), and B–O (194.5 eV) [41]. In addition, N 1s (Fig. 1i) involves that the majority of the N in the CB-I takes the form of N–H. The above results confirmed the analysis was in agreement with the FT-IR and demonstrated the existence of glassy boric acid and the successful doping of boron and oxygen in the carbon dots.

In addition, the origin of the dual-mode (DF and RTP) afterglow of CB-I was further confirmed by the temperature-dependent decay curves at 438 nm and 490 nm respectively. Firstly, the proportions of delayed components at 438 nm enhance with the increase of temperature from 230 K to 290 K (Fig. 3 a, Table S4). The RISC process is largely facilitated when the temperature rises, which is the typical feature of delayed fluorescence [31,46].Moreover, the average afterglow lifetime of DF is 0.8 s at 230 K and then drops as the temperature rises. Secondly, CB-I has excellent phosphorescence properties as predicted after monitoring the temperature-dependent decay curves at 490 nm (Fig. 3b). The vibration can quench largely triplet excitons and accelerate the non-radiative transition, resulting in the reduction of the lifetime as the temperature goes up.

Besides, there seems to be other evidence to indicate the dual-mode afterglow is carried out. As shown in Fig. 3c, the electron first transits from the ground state (S0) to the excited state (Sn) after being excited, and then return to the S1 state. The highly rigid boric acid matrix, new B–C bonds, and other effective limiting factors cause the S1 state to drop to the T1 state as a result of the effective promotion of ISC by C=O and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the carbon dots surface. This suppresses the non-radiative transition and results in room-temperature phosphorescence. In addition, the energy gap (ΔE ST) between the lowest triplet excited state (T1) and the lowest singlet excited state (S1) is calculated to be 0.30 eV, which is sufficient to promote the reverse intersystem crossing, as evidenced by the fluorescence and afterglow emission spectrum of CB-I at 77 K given in Fig. S3 [29]. Collectively, all of the studies reviewed here support the hypothesis above that low temperature is profitable to phosphorescence, a relatively higher temperature is beneficial to delayed fluorescence, and efficient DF and RTP dual-mode afterglow emission can be achieved at ambient conditions.

Impressively, the phosphorescence displays the maximum single-mode afterglow lifetime of 1.11 s at 80 K (Fig. 3d), which can be observed with the naked eye for 11 s (Fig. 3e). [] Afterward, the conversion from dual-mode DF and RTP afterglow emission to single-mode phosphorescence afterglow emission can be realized by altering the temperature. Based on this, the afterglow of CB-I displays a significant color change from deep blue to cyan with decreasing temperature from 323 K to 80 K owing to the gradual attenuation of delayed fluorescence and the enhancement of phosphorescence (Fig. 3f). CB-Ⅰ could be applied as temperature-sensitive materials for multi-level anti-counterfeiting and information encryption for its distinctive color-tunable afterglow property. Currently, biological applications, temperature sensing, and other fields mostly employ a single fluorescence or phosphorescence signal change for exploration. However, various limitations and shortcomings have been exposed that hinder further research and development. The discussion of the temperature-responsive color-tunable afterglow from dual-mode delayed fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence to single-mode phosphorescence is realized in this work, which is practical for future studies with a highly favorable impact.

Meanwhile, the CB-I composites possess superior stability. As shown in Fig. S4a, the TGA curve of the CB-I composites reveals the carbon contents lose just 2.5% of their weight when the temperature reaches 100 °C, indicating the CB-I maintains excellent thermal stability at 100 °C. In addition, the stability of CB-Ⅰ in the air at ambient conditions was determined by measuring the delayed emission spectrum of CB-Ⅰ in its original and air for 14 days. As depicted in Fig. S4b, the afterglow emission intensity of the sample placed in air for 14 days only slightly decreased and still retained 92% of the original, thus further verifying that CB-Ⅰ still has excellent stability under the protection of the boric acid matrix. Moreover, the afterglow also possesses good thermal stability. The blue afterglow was still detectable at 65 °C with the naked eye, although the afterglow was considerably less intense than it was at 25 °C (323 K) (Fig. S4c). Although the afterglow of CB-I is no longer discernible at 100 °C, it is commendable that the faint blue transient afterglow can still be observed at 80 °C, demonstrating that CB-I not only has positive air and thermal stability but also retains its afterglow characteristics when heated to 80 °C which has excellent practical applications prospects.

Based on the above discussion, CB-I was synthesized via the hydrothermal treatment of ciprofloxacin and boric acid to capture the bright afterglow. Firstly, ciprofloxacin, a drug with broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity and good bactericidal efficacy, is rich in C=O functional groups [47] which are widely considered to be the basis for the generation of carbon dots afterglow emission. Secondly, thanks to the multiple roles played by the molten glassy state boric acid [48] at high temperatures, the matrix network with high rigidity can form a protective structure to inhibit the non-radiative transition of carbon dots by limiting the vibrational movement of molecules and isolating the quenching of oxygen (Fig. S5). Therefore, the high-efficiency temperature-responsive color-tunable single-mode and dual-mode afterglow composites were realized with purple light excitable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphological and photophysical characterization of controlled composites
ce:section.ce:para: A series of starting materials were screened and the controlled experiments presented were carried out to delve deeper into the dual-mode and single-mole afterglow emission of carbon dots composites. CB-II, CB-III, CB-IV, CB-V, CB-VI, and CB-VII composites could be produced with a fixed amount of boric acid matrix by altering the concentration of ciprofloxacin. Their X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns share the same primary characteristic peaks as CB-I (Fig. S6). The surface chemical structures of controlled experiments were investigated by combining Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Each of the six composites has characteristic absorption bands in their FTIR spectrum that resemble CB-I (Fig. S7a). In addition, the comparison of the FTIR spectrum from CB-II to CB-VII shows the characteristic peaks of ciprofloxacin gradually obvious since the amount of ciprofloxacin continuously increases, which further indicates that the existence the different amounts of ciprofloxacin carbon dots in the composites (Fig. S7b). The FTIR results from CB-II to CB-VII are further validated by XPS. As presented in Figs. S8a–f and Table S5, the percentage of carbon content progressively improves with the increase of ciprofloxacin, as C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, C=O, and O–C=O dominantly showing the trend of rising from 26.10% to 44.41%. Additionally, the elevation in the ratio of carbon dots in the C 1s XPS spectrum renders the peak of O–C=O apparent. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s (Figs. S9a–f) confirms the presence of C=O, C–O–C/C–O–B, and O–C=O/C–OH comparable to CB-I composites. BCO2, B2O3, and B–O bonds could all be assigned to the three components that comprise B 1s (Figs. S10a–f). The N–H bond is identified as the main component in the N 1s XPS spectrum (Figs. S11a–f). However, N is no longer only in the form of graphitic nitrogen as it was in CB-I but has instead altered to a mixture composed of C–N=C (pyridine N) and graphitic nitrogen [49,50] with the increase in ciprofloxacin to 175 mg. The primary factor is that when ciprofloxacin gets to a certain level, the distinctive peak of pyridine nitrogen in the composites manifests itself distinctly and won't appear at lower ratios. In all the studies reviewed here, boron oxide and the co-doping of boron and oxygen are recognized as existing in the carbon dots.

When ciprofloxacin is incorporated into the boric acid will it show the afterglow, while boric acid and ciprofloxacin by themselves produce no afterglow at ambient conditions (Figs. S12a–b). [51] Given the lack of rigid protection and quench in aqueous, the afterglow disappears when CB-I is dissolved in water and only emits blue fluorescence with no afterglow phenomenon (Fig. S13a) [52].Although the phosphorescence properties of ciprofloxacin powder were not observed at ambient conditions, it was surprising to see that the delayed spectrum at 77 K showed a distinct emission peak of ciprofloxacin at 491 nm (Fig. S13b), which was in complete agreement with the phosphorescence emission peak of CB-I at 490 nm at ambient conditions and further confirmed that the phosphorescent luminescence of CB-I came from ciprofloxacin luminophore. In addition, the temperature-dependent delayed emission spectrum revealed that the intensity of the afterglow spectrum of ciprofloxacin powder decreased with temperature increase, which confirmed its phosphorescence properties (Fig. S13c). The decay curve of ciprofloxacin at 77 K was also measured and further fitted to the lifetime of 476 ms (Fig. S13d). Therefore, ciprofloxacin powder possesses an afterglow phenomenon at 77 K and has an afterglow lifetime of 476 ms. The limiting effect of the boric acid matrix further optimizes the afterglow performance and achieves dual-mode afterglow emission at ambient conditions, further emphasizing the important role of the boric acid protective matrix. Considering all of the evidence with the above FTIR, XPS, and other characterizations, the existence of B–O bonds can be confirmed and the importance of the boric acid matrix to the afterglow emission can be inferred.

The luminescence properties of CB-II, CB-III, CB-IV, CB-V, CB-VI, and CB-VII were also recorded. They exhibit bright fluorescence and afterglow (Fig. 4 a) which afterglow persists for 0.5–5 s under ambient conditions after the UV lamp is removed. The afterglow of CB-II to CB-VII shows a color change to red-shift from bottom to top under the purple visible light excitation of the 422 nm (Fig. 2f). The absorption spectrum (Fig. S14) demonstrates that there was also an absorption peak in the range of 300 nm–400 nm similar to CB-I and red-shifted with ciprofloxacin increasing. This indicates that the conjugation system improves, and the absorption peak moves to the lower wavenumber direction, resulting in a larger red shift as the amount of ciprofloxacin carbon dots increases. Correspondingly, the maximum peak of the fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. 4b) and afterglow emission spectrum (Fig. 4c) from CB-II to CB-VII progressively move in the direction of the long wavelength. The overall fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S1, Table S1) of these composites featured a rising trend and the PLQY of CB-II to CB-VII were 68.63%, 73.49%, 65.87%, 68.12%, 70.01%, and 67.25%, respectively, all of which were over 65%. In addition, the afterglow quantum yield of CB-IV under excitation of 422 nm in the purple light region was obtained at 75 mg of ciprofloxacin was 32.41%, and when the amount of ciprofloxacin was 725 mg, the afterglow quantum yield reached 40.97%. Such a high afterglow quantum yield is very rare in the study of dual-mode afterglow materials (Fig. S2, Table 1).

Furthermore, composites from CB-II to CB-VII displayed a typical excitation-independent phenomenon in the fluorescence emission spectrum (Figs. S15a–f) and the afterglow emission spectrum (Figs. S16a–f). The temperature-dependent afterglow emission spectrum (Figs. S17a–f) of the six composites was monitored to further explore whether the synthetic control experiments also exhibit dual-mode afterglow. Unsurprisingly, all composites represented dual-mode afterglow characteristics and the intensity of phosphorescence progressively weakened and the intensity of delayed fluorescence gradually increased with the rise in temperature, which is exactly the characteristics of phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence. Based on the above results, the afterglow decay curves of the six comparative samples at the positions belonging to the delayed fluorescence peak and the room temperature phosphorescence peak were also measured (Fig. 4d and e). The average afterglow lifetime as expectancy has declined since the boric acid network effectively restricts the luminescent groups and suppresses the quenching of triplet excitons at low ciprofloxacin masses. As the mass of ciprofloxacin grows, it becomes evident that boric acid is become more ineffective in limiting the luminescent groups of ciprofloxacin and resulting in a drop in the afterglow lifetime. Specific average lifetime values of CB-II to CB-VII are listed in Table S2 and Table S3, respectively.

The carbon dots composites from CB-I to CB-VII were further employed with high-efficiency luminescence and distinguishable afterglow lifetime for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting technology. As shown in Fig. 5 a, seven powders from CB-I to CB-VII were evenly coated to pre-prepared glass sheets with uniform length and width, and the photographs of their different afterglow lifetimes were recorded in sunlight and under eliminated 365 nm irradiation. Based on this, a multi-layer information encryption code with CB-I and CB-IV powders is first simply designed (Fig. 5b). Only the white powder pattern of the four numbers “8″ appeared in sunlight, that is “false information” was obtained. Under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, the white powder numbers emitted bright blue fluorescence and created a second “false information”, which amounts to an additional layer of information encryption as it is hidden and can only be recognized when excited by UV light. Once the excitation stopped, only “HELP” can be observed since CB-I possesses different afterglow decay rates that are slower than that of CB-IV powder over time. Therefore, the real information is just accessed once the second layer of information encryption has been completed. These results suggest that these long-lived and highly efficient composites exhibit broad applications in information encryption.

In addition, another novel anti-counterfeiting application based on absorbing data has been expanded. Fig. 5c shows the afterglow luminescence map of the composites. A smiling face pattern is created by setting the “eyebrow” and “left eye” with CB-I powder, “right eye” with CB-V powder, and “nose” with CB-VII at corresponding positions. The CB-VII powder has the shortest lifetime over time and the “nose” built with CB-VII in the smiley pattern disappears in less than a second due to the large difference in decay rates of the three composites. After another layer of time screening, the afterglow of CB-V disappeared one after another, leaving behind a smiling face pattern with blinking eyes. A smiley face pattern with “bending eyes” is displayed when the time further reaches the CB-I powder with the longest lifetimes. The color darker as the intensity of afterglow luminescence declines. When the UV lamp is opened again to excite and turn off the UV light, the complete smiling face pattern is restored. To sum up, the discoveries presented in this section indicate that the composites have great potential for applications in advanced encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, the high-efficiency temperature-responsive color-tunable dual-mode afterglow and single-mode carbon dot composites excited by purple light in the visible region have been successfully realized. Additionally, carbon dots composites with long-lived afterglow and ultra-high afterglow quantum yields of 193.5 ms and 35.07% were obtained by embedding the luminescent centers of ciprofloxacin into the formed rigid boric acid matrix by a one-step hydrothermal with purple light as the excitation wavelength. The feature of the afterglow is determined by the optimal spatial confinement which effectively suppresses molecular vibration and stabilizes the triplet state excitons of carbon dots. Moreover, the afterglow quantum yield of the controlled experiment in CB-VII is as high as 40.97%. It is noteworthy that the constructed composites display dual-mode delayed fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence afterglow properties at ambient conditions, which possesses distinctive afterglow emission dominated by delayed fluorescence or room temperature phosphorescence at different temperatures and enables color-tunable afterglow emission with temperature-sensitivity. Due to its exceptional temperature sensitivity, ultra-long single-mode phosphorescence afterglow can be achieved under 80 K with a 1.11 s afterglow lifetime, realizing the conversion of temperature-responsive from dual-mode afterglow to single-mode afterglow. Furthermore, the composites are also applied in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications owing to their excellent and stable performance.

Jingran Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology. Xiulan Wu: Formal analysis. Xin Jiang: Data curation, Visualization. Ming Li: Supervision. Rongxing He: Supervision. Wei Shen: Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Beibei Science and Technology Talents and Independent Innovation Special Project (No. 2021-5).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2023.03.048.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The room temperature afterglow mechanism in carbon dots: current state and further guidance perspective
Carbon
